Basic information about this patient, female, fifty-five years old, BMI is twenty-five. Colonoscopy and pathological biopsy, show that there is a well differentiated adenocarcinoma at the dentate line.

and its maximum diameter is two point five centimeter Miles operation was proposed but this patient asked for retailing her anus or not accept this operation after trade-off analysis intersphincteric resection ISR was impelled and we must face one challenge reservation on defecation function next we show the surgical video
The laparoscopic view reveals the absence of intraperitoneal metastases, indicating no visible spread of cancer within the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon opens the sigmoid mesocolon on the right side and extends the incision towards the horizontal part of the duodenum, creating an entrance into the retrorectal space.

The surgeon carefully separates the space while adhering to the rectal proper fascia, ensuring the inferior hypogastric plexus within the subperitoneal fascia is preserved. After completing the separation, the surgeon moves to the gaps along the incision in the sigmoid mesocolon, exposing and ligating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) in this area.

The surgeon extends the dissection space laterally, revealing the superior hypogastric plexus situated dorsally. The testicular blood vessel and ureter are carefully dissected from the surrounding tissue within the retrorectal space.

Gauze is placed into the separated gap to guide the dissection route on the lateral peritoneum. The surgeon clearly dissects the lateral peritoneum along the trijunction, fully freeing the sigmoid colon and descending colon.

The surgeon makes an incision above the peritoneal reflection, entering the anterior rectal space. The pelvic plexus is visible and requires attention as the rectal collateral ligament is transversed.

The surgeon continues to separate along the rectal proper fascia until reaching the end of the Denon-Villers fascia. At this level, the rectal proper fascia is absent, and the surgeon begins to open the muscle rectum.

Under laparoscopic view, the surgeon separates the rectal and pelvic muscles further, focusing on the puborectalis muscle attached to the rectum. The rectum is carefully manipulated to facilitate its division.
The surgeon continues to bear the rectum, entering the anal sphincter space at the edge of the levator ani muscle. Gauze is used to guide the transanal approach route.

The laparoscopic separation is completed, and the focus shifts to the transanal approach. After dilating the anus, an anus retractor is used to provide a clear view. The tumor is located on the posterior wall of the rectum, with no invasion into the anterior wall. A wedge resection is planned using a semi-pouch suture technique to preserve the rectal mucous membrane receptor.
The surgeon excises the rectal mucosa and enters the anal sphincter gap, continuing the transanal approach as demonstrated in the diagram.

The surgeon pulls the colon out through the anus and makes a cut five centimeters away from the tumor. The colon and rectum are sewn together in stage one using a circular stapler. The circular suture at the distal intestine anal retractor is loosened before knotting. The integrity of the margin is checked and sent for frozen pathology. The anastomosis is reinforced with interrupted sutures to prevent leakage.
The surgeon irrigates the abdominal cavity thoroughly and places a drainage tube in the pelvic cavity under laparoscopic guidance before concluding the operation.
